coredata.dc:title: Ultra-stable carbon quantum dot nanofluids as excellent spectral beam splitters in PV/T applications
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Yang
Bao, Yanqiong
Yu, Linfeng
Zheng, Xiong
Qin, Guangzhao
Chen, Meijie
He, Maogang
coredata.dc:description: Nanofluids have potential application as spectral beam splitters (SBSs) in photovoltaic/thermal (PV/T) systems due to their tunable spectral absorption properties, while current nanofluid SBSs have some disadvantages, which limit their large-scale application in practice. In this work, carbon quantum dot (CQD) nanofluids have been proven to be excellent SBSs in PV/T systems. CQD-nanofluid filters can be synthesized by a simple microwave heating method and show ultra-stability for a long period. Meanwhile, the optical property of CQD nanofluids was fine-tuned by changing the heating time and PEG concentration. The absorption of nanofluids can be enhanced in the whole spectrum by increasing heating time, and the absorption of nanofluids to specific wavelengths can be regulated by adjusting the PEG concentration. The nanofluid filter with 50% PEG concentration and 20 min heating time shows the best performance with a merit function (MF) value of 1.904 when the worth factor (w) is 3, which outperforms the reported nanofluid filters in the literature. This work paves the way for the application of ultra-stable CQD nanofluids as SBSs for large-scale utilization in solar energy power generation stations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Nanofluids
Photovoltaic/thermal system
Spectral beam splitters

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of CQD nanofluid filters in PV/T application
Morphology and stability of CQD nanofluids
PV cell characteristics filtered by nanofluids

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52006059 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram of the PV/T performance assessment system.

Morphology and stability of CQD nanofluids. (a) Physical pictures of CQD nanofluids with different heating times; (b) TEM image of CQD-50-4 nanofluid; (c) Average size of CQD nanofluids as a function of heating time; (c-1) Size distribution of CQDs in CQD-50-4; (d) Physical pictures of CQD nanofluids standing for 30 days; (e) Transmittance curves of CQD nanofluids freshly prepared and standing for 30 days; (f) Zeta potential of CQDs as a function of different heating times.
Transmittance curves of CQD nanofluids with (a) different heating times and (b) different PEG concentrations with a heating time of 20 min.

I–V characteristics of PV cell with CQD nanofluid filters with different PEG concentrations (a) and different heating times (c); Electrical efficiencies of PV cell with CQD nanofluid filters with different PEG concentrations (b) and different heating times (d).

Temperature rises of CQD nanofluid filters with different PEG concentrations (a) and different heating times (c); Thermal efficiencies of CQD nanofluid filters with different PEG concentrations (b) and different heating times (d).

MF values, electrical and thermal efficiencies in PV/T systems with nanofluid filters at different PEG concentrations (a) and different heating times (b) when w = 3. (c) Comparison of MF and efficiency between literary works and our results.
Details of the prepared CQD nanofluids.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: c 1 is the concentration of PEG-200 in the solutions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: MF values, electrical and thermal efficiencies in PV/T systems with nanofluid filters at different PEG concentrations and different heating times when w = 3.
Comparison of MF and efficiency between literary works and our results.

head.ce:title: Ultra-stable carbon quantum dot nanofluids as excellent spectral beam splitters in PV/T applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Thermo-Fluid Science and Engineering, Ministry of Education, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, Shaanxi Province, 710049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Thermo-Fluid Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Thermo-Fluid Science and Engineering, Ministry of Education, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, Shaanxi Province, 710049, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanofluids have potential application as spectral beam splitters (SBSs) in photovoltaic/thermal (PV/T) systems due to their tunable spectral absorption properties, while current nanofluid SBSs have some disadvantages, which limit their large-scale application in practice. In this work, carbon quantum dot (CQD) nanofluids have been proven to be excellent SBSs in PV/T systems. CQD-nanofluid filters can be synthesized by a simple microwave heating method and show ultra-stability for a long period. Meanwhile, the optical property of CQD nanofluids was fine-tuned by changing the heating time and PEG concentration. The absorption of nanofluids can be enhanced in the whole spectrum by increasing heating time, and the absorption of nanofluids to specific wavelengths can be regulated by adjusting the PEG concentration. The nanofluid filter with 50% PEG concentration and 20 min heating time shows the best performance with a merit function (MF) value of 1.904 when the worth factor (w) is 3, which outperforms the reported nanofluid filters in the literature. This work paves the way for the application of ultra-stable CQD nanofluids as SBSs for large-scale utilization in solar energy power generation stations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The consumption of fossil fuels causes serious environmental pollution and energy crisis, and the development of renewable energy becomes a promising way to solve these problems [1–5]. As an important member of renewable energy, solar energy is regarded as an ideal alternative to fossil fuels on the merit of its environment-friendly and abundant characteristics [6–9], and various advanced technologies have been employed in the utilization of solar energy [10,11]. Among these technologies, the commercial promotion of photovoltaic (PV) technology is the most successful. While in traditional PV systems, the cells can only respond to a specific range of the solar spectrum, and the rest irradiance can only be converted into heat and induces the temperature rise of cells, which causes a decrease in electrical efficiency and even damage to PV cells due to thermal stress [12,13]. For example, the response spectrum of silicon PV cells is generally from 650 nm to 1050 nm and the electrical efficiency of the current silicon PV cells is usually lower than 25% [14,15].

To reduce the operating temperature of PV cells, cooling methods are usually introduced into PV modules, including water cooling, air cooling, and phase change material cooling [16–18]. In cooling systems, heat transfer fluids (HTFs) can absorb the heat on PV cells and convert it to the required place, so the total energy efficiency of the system can be improved compared with pure PV cells. This system is called the traditional photovoltaic/thermal (PV/T) system. In this traditional system, the output temperature of HTF is usually as low as 310–320 K with an energy efficiency of 15% due to the limitation of PV cells’ temperature [19], and the utilization of this low-grade heat is limited in the areas of hot water supply, heat pump, building heating, etc. [20].

Under such a circumstance, a novel PV/T system containing a spectral beam splitter (SBS) was invented to solve this problem fundamentally. An ideal SBS can filter out the light not needed by PV cells and only let the light desired to pass through, which can be separated into a selective absorption fluid filter and a thin-film interference filter, and the former filter is cheaper than the latter one [21]. The concept of SBS was originally proposed by Jackson in 1955 [22] and conducted experimentally by Moonetal et al. in 1978 [23]. Pure fluids, like water and oil, were originally used in SBS, while pure fluids have weak absorption for ultra-violet light and can only absorb a portion of infrared light, so they do not perform well in PV/T systems. It is necessary to find new fluid filters with better performance to improve the total efficiency of PV/T systems.

Recently, nanofluids containing nanoparticles have been proven to be effective filters in PV/T systems on the advantage of their regulable optical properties. In literature, several works have been carried out to improve the performance of PV/T systems using nanofluid filters with a dispersion of various nanoparticles, including mental oxide nanoparticles, plasmonic nanoparticles, core-shell nanoparticles, etc. Cui et al. investigated the performance of MgO/water nanofluid filters on silicon cell, which can achieve a thermal efficiency of 47.2% with a small electrical efficiency decrease in PV cell [24]. An et al. carried out the study of polypyrrole/water and Cu9S5/oleylamine, the overall efficiencies are 25.1 and 34.2%, respectively [25,26]. Meanwhile, the performance of plasmonic nanoparticles was checked, such as Ag [27] and Au [28] nanoparticles, show relatively weak absorption for ultra-violet light. Furthermore, a type of more complicated core-shell structures, such as Ag@SiO2 [29], Ag@TiO2 [30], and their hybrid nanoparticles [31], have been extensively introduced into nanofluid filters, the performance of PV/T systems has been significantly improved due to the excellent optical properties of core-shell nanoparticles.

From the review of the literary works, we can see that some nanofluid filters can achieve high thermal and electric efficiencies simultaneously, while there are still some challenges for the large-scale application of nanofluid filters in practice. First, the cost of nanofluids is high, like Ag and Au nanoparticles, which creates obstacles to their extensive use in industries. Second, sophisticated processes are usually involved in the manufacturing of nanoparticles, such as core-shell structures, which also makes large-scale production of nanofluids difficult. Third, stability has always been one of the crucial problems of nanofluids, which constraints their long-term use. So it is of great significance to explore new nanofluids more suitable for practical application.

Based on this target, we creatively introduce simple and low-cost carbon quantum dot (CQD) nanofluids with excellent stability and selective spectral absorption performance into PV/T systems. Carbon quantum dot (CQD) nanofluids have the advantages of simple production processes, low cost, and good solar absorption ability and have received tremendous attention in catalysis, medicine, energy absorption, etc. [32–35]. In this study, it is found that CQD nanofluids containing polyethylene glycol (PEG) and glycerol (GL) show low absorption for the spectrum from 650 to 1050 nm and high absorption at other wavelengths, which is exactly desired by ideal SBSs in PV/T systems. The preparation of CQD nanofluids is simple and the cost can be effectively controlled, which is very helpful for their wide applications. This study will provide great guidance for the practical development of PV/T technology.

Polyethylene glycol (PEG, M w = 200) and glycerol (GL) were supplied by Aladdin. The purities of PEG 200 and GL were 98% and 99% in mass, respectively, and no further purification was adopted before use.

CQD nanofluids were prepared by the microwave-heating method. Dipole materials can absorb microwaves, which causes local heating of the samples [36]. The preparation processes of CQD nanofluids are shown in Fig. 1 . First, the samples were weighed using an electronic balance with an uncertainty of 0.1 mg (Mettler Toledo, ME104). Then, the weighed samples were mixed in a beaker and stirred using a magnetic stirrer (Lichen, LC-MSH-10L) for 30 min. Finally, the precursor solution was heated for a certain period in the microwave oven with a maximum power of 700 W (Xiaomi, MWBLXE1ACM). In this way, CQD nanofluids can be successfully synthesized.

In this work, different heating times and PEG concentrations of precursor solutions were adopted in the preparation of CQD nanofluids, and the heating times and PEG concentrations of CQD nanofluids are listed in Table 1 .

The morphology of CQDs was observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, Talos F200S Super-X). In TEM observation, CQD-50-4 was used to prepare the TEM sample, which was diluted 100 times with deionized water to have a better observation of CQDs. Meanwhile, the size distributions of CQDs were analyzed by a DLS setup (Malvern, Zetasizer Nano ZS).

Stability is one of the most important properties of nanofluids, which affects the long-term performance of nanofluids. In this work, the stability of CQD nanofluids was accessed in three ways. First, the Zeta potentials of CQD nanofluids were measured by a Zeta potentiometer (Malvern, Zetasizer Nano ZS). Second, the amount of precipitation was observed by the naked eye when the nanofluids stand for 30 days. Third, the transmittance changes between the samples freshly made and put for 30 days were analyzed. The transmittance of CQD nanofluids was determined by a UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer (Hitachi, U4100) at wavelengths of 200–2000 nm with an interval of 5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of CQD nanofluid filters in PV/T application
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the performance of CQD nanofluid filters was tested using a PV/T performance assessment system, and the diagram of this system is illustrated in Fig. 2 . A commercial silicon PV cell (Sunshine Science, YG3030) with a dimension of 25 × 25 mm2 was used in this system, which was sensitive to the solar irradiance between 650 and 1050 nm. The prepared CQD nanofluids were used as optical filters, and the thickness of the nanofluid coating is 10 mm. A xenon lamp (Aulight Inc., CEL-HXF300) was used as the solar simulator, and the intensity of light was kept at one sun during experiments. An I–V analyzer (TES Electrical Electronic Corp., PROVA-200A) was used to measure the I–V characteristics of the PV cell, and the resolutions for the current and voltage measurements are 0.1 mA and 0.001 V, respectively. The temperatures of nanofluid filters and PV cells were recorded with an interval of 5 s by infrared thermal imager (UNI-T, UTi320, uncertainty is 0.2 K) and platinum resistance (Omega, PR-10, uncertainty is 0.2 K), respectively.

Before the measurement, the uncertainty of the PV/T system was estimated to ensure that the measured data are reliable. The uncertainty of the PV/T system consists of five contributions, electrical power from the I–V analyzer, the strength of solar irradiance from the solar simulator, the area of irradiance from Vernier caliper, the temperature of nanofluids from the infrared thermal imager, and the mass of nanofluids from electrical balance. The combined uncertainty of the PV/T system is calculated as
(1)
U c
=
∑
i = 1
5
u i 2
where U c is the combined uncertainty of the system, u i is the uncertainty from five different contributions, which are listed in Table 2 . The combined uncertainty of the system is calculated as 3.6%. In repeated experiments, the error of each measurement is within 3.0%, which indicates that our experimental results are reliable and acceptable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and stability of CQD nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of CQD nanofluids, the heating time determines the degree of carbonization. Fig. 3 (a) presents the physical pictures of prepared CQD nanofluids with different heating times from 10 to 40 min, which shows that the longer time the samples are heated, the darker the color is. The TEM image of CQDs in nanofluids is shown in Fig. 3(b). Although the CQDs are usually difficult to be observed by TEM, our image still relatively clearly shows the CQD nanoparticles. The fabricated CQD nanoparticles are approximately spherical, and CQDs separate from each other very well, which indicates that CQD nanofluids have good dispersion quality. Fig. 3(b) also indicates that the size distribution of CQDs is very uniform. Besides, to further analyze the size of CQDs, the diameter distribution was analyzed by a DLS setup as a function of heating times, and the results of CQD-50 nanofluid are shown in Fig. 3(c). When the heating time is 10 min, the average size of CQDs is about 7 nm. Besides, the average size increases with the heating time, and the average size increases to 13 nm when the heating time is 40 min. Meanwhile, Figs. 3(c-1) shows that the particle size distribution is a normal distribution centered on the average value, which is consistence with literature results [29,33].

Besides, the stability of nanofluids is crucial for the long-term application of working fluids, here three methods are adopted to evaluate the stability of CQD nanofluids. First, the sedimentation of CQDs was detected after the samples stood for 30 days (shown in Fig. 3(d)), and little sedimentation was found at the bottom of the bottles. Second, transmittance curves were compared between the samples freshly made and standing for 30 days (shown in Fig. 3(e)), the transmittance change is within 2.0%. Third, the absolute Zeta potential was measured for CQD nanofluids with different heating times (shown in Fig. 3(f)). Zeta potential has been regarded as an important evaluating indicator of the stability of nanofluids, and the nanofluid with an absolute value higher than 30 mV is generally considered to be stable. Fig. 3(f) indicates that all CQD nanofluids have Zeta potential values significantly higher than 30 mV, which means that the prepared CQD nanofluids have extraordinary stability. Furthermore, the value decreases with the heating time, indicating that the heating time will weaken the stability, and this negative effect may be attributed to the growth of CQD size with the increase of heating time because larger nanoparticles are easier to precipitate.

In CQD nanofluids, both heating time and PEG concentration affect their optical properties, so the transmittance of CQD nanofluids at different heating times and PEG concentrations were measured from 200 to 2000 nm, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . It can be seen from Fig. 4 that CQD nanofluids can let the light in a wavelength of 650–1050 nm pass through and significantly absorb the light at other wavelengths, which is exactly what the ideal SBS of silicon PV cell requires. Fig. 4(a) shows that the transmittance of CQD nanofluids decreases with the heating time in the whole spectrum, so there exists an optimized heating time to make the PV/T system perform best. To analyze the influence of PEG concentration on the optical properties of CQD nanofluids, the transmittance of CQD nanofluids at different PEG concentrations with a heating time of 20 min were measured, and the results are shown in Fig. 4(b). It can be seen that the spectrum of CQD nanofluids can be adjusted effectively by changing the mass ratio of PEG and GL. The addition of GL improves the transmittance in the wavelengths between 650 and 1050 nm, which is favorable for nanofluid filters. While too much GL also weakens the absorption in the ultraviolet region, there also exists an optimized GL concentration to maximize the efficiency of the PV/T system.

Besides, CQDs can absorb the photons with low energy and convert them into photons with high energy, that is, the long wave can be converted into the short wave, which is known as photoluminescence. Usually, the maximum emission wavelength is lower than 500 nm. The response region of Si cells is from 650 to 1050 nm, so the emission wavelength of CQDs is outside the response wavelength region of Si cells, which means that quantum yields of CQDs have a negative effect on the performance as filters and we need to restrain quantum yields of CQDs. While the quantum yields are very small in usual, it is expected that the influence of this effect on the performance of CQD nanofluids in the PV/T system is limited.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PV cell characteristics filtered by nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: After we analyzed the optical properties of CQD nanofluids, we assessed the performance of PV/T systems with nanofluid filters using the setup presented in Fig. 1. The I–V characteristics of PV cells filtered by nanofluid filters with different PEG concentrations are shown in Fig. 5 (a). The short circuit current (I sc) of PV cells can be significantly weakened with the existence of nanofluid filters, which is because of the absorption of the incident light by nanofluid filters. While the open circuit voltage (V oc) changes little by nanofluid filters, which is attributed to the insensitivity of V oc to the strength of incident light.

Furthermore, the electrical efficiencies (η pv) of PV cells were obtained to discuss the performance of CQD nanofluid filters in the PV/T system. η pv is calculated as
(2)
η pv
=
V opt
⋅
I opt
G ⋅ A ⋅ Δ t
in which V opt and I opt are the voltage and current of the PV cell at maximum power, respectively, which are gotten determined from the I–V curves of the PV cell. G is the light intensity of the solar simulator; and A is the illuminating area of the nanofluid filters, which is 12.56 cm2.

The electrical efficiencies (η pv) of PV cells at different PEG concentrations are shown in Fig. 5(b), which indicates that η pv decreases monotonically over PEG concentration. η pv of the PV cell is as high as 15.8% without a nanofluid filter, while decreases to 5.4% using a CQD-100 filter, which is about 1/3 of that of the PV cell without a filter. The addition of PEG in the precursor solution results in the increase of CQDs after heating, so more light can be absorbed by CQD nanofluids, which is the reason why η pv decreases with the increase of PEG concentration.

Meanwhile, the influence of nanofluids’ heating time on the electrical efficiency of PV cells is also analyzed, the I–V curves of PV cell under the filter with different heating time are shown in Fig. 5(c). Similar to the effect of PEG concentration, heating time rarely influence V oc of PV cells, while the increase of heating time weakens I sc. Which results in the decrease of η pv (shown in Fig. 5(d)). η pv decreases from 12.9% to 9.6% when the heating time increases from 10 to 40 min. Longer heating time induces higher level of carbonization, so CQD nanofluids with longer heating time have higher light absorption ability, which causes the decrease of η pv.

The above analysis indicates that part of the electrical efficiency will be sacrificed with the existence of nanofluid filters, while a considerable part of the solar irradiation is converted into heat energy, which may induce the increase of the total efficiency containing both electrical and thermal efficiencies, so the thermal efficiency of CQD nanofluid filters is also analyzed to obtain a more comprehensive analysis of total efficiency of PV/T system. To assess the thermal efficiency of filters, the temperature rise of nanofluid filters was recorded as a function of time. The influence of PEG concentration on the temperature rise of CQD nanofluids is shown in Fig. 6 (a). The temperature rise of nanofluid filters increases with PEG concentration, which means that the addition of PEG enhances the thermal efficiency of nanofluid filters. After obtaining the temperature rise of nanofluids, the thermal efficiency of CQD nanofluid filters was calculated as [37].
(3)
η th
=
c f
m f
Δ T
G ⋅ A ⋅ Δ t
where m f is the mass of nanofluids; c f is the heat capacity of nanofluids, here the amount of CQDs is low, so the heat capacity of nanofluids is approximately considered to be equivalent to that of base fluids [34], which is calculated using literature data [38,39]; ΔT is the temperature rise of nanofluids under irradiation, which is measured directly in our experiments; Δt is the illuminating time, which is chosen as the initial 600 s to avoid the influence of heat loss at high temperature. During the measurements, the filters are relatively thin (10 mm), so the temperature gradient inside the filters can be neglected and the temperature distribution can be regarded to be uniform under irradiation. Fig. 6(b) presents the relationship between the thermal efficiency of CQD nanofluids and PEG concentration. In pure GL, the thermal efficiency of the nanofluid filter is 33.9%, and the thermal efficiency increases to 49.8% when the PEG concentration is 100%. The thermal efficiency of CQD nanofluids has been improved by half when PEG concentration increases from 0 to 100%. Contrary to the effect on electrical efficiency, the addition of PEG has a positive effect on the thermal conductivity of filters, which is also attributed to the enhancement of light absorption ability with the addition of PEG.

Besides, the influence of heating time on the thermal efficiency of nanofluid filters is also analyzed. The temperature rise of CQD nanofluid (PEG concentration is 50%) with four different heating times is illustrated in Fig. 6(c). Longer heating time means a higher level of carbonization, which results in a higher temperature rise of nanofluids under solar irradiance. Correspondingly, the thermal efficiency of nanofluid filters also increases with the heating time, the thermal efficiency of nanofluid increases from 40.0% to 49.2% when the heating time increases from 10 to 40 min.

From the above analysis, we can see that the electrical efficiency of PV cell decreases with the PEG concentration or heating time of nanofluid filters, while the thermal efficiency of nanofluid filters shows an opposite trend, so there exists an optimized nanofluid filter that can achieve the best solar energy utilization in PV/T system. To assess the performance of nanofluid filters more comprehensively, the merit function (MF) is introduced to evaluate the performance of nanofluid filters considering both electrical and thermal efficiencies. The formula for MF calculation reads [40].
(4)
MF =
w ⋅
η pv
+
η th
w ⋅
η
pv , unfiltered
where w is the worth factor of electrical energy to thermal energy, which reflects the relative values of electricity and heat and is chosen as 3 considering the local prices of heat and electricity; η pv is the electrical efficiency of PV cells with the existence of filters, which is obtained from the I–V characteristics; η pv,unfiltered is the electrical efficiency of PV cell without filter, which is 15.80% according to our experimental results (see Table 3 ). The calculated MF values as well as the thermal efficiency of nanofluid filters, electrical efficiency of PV cells, and total energy efficiency are presented in Table 3 and Fig. 7 . The MF values as a function of PEG concentration are shown in Fig. 7(a), the relationship between MF and PEG concentration is a parabola function and the inflection point occurs at 50% PEG concentration, where the value of MF is 1.904 and the total efficiency is 56.1%. When PEG concentration is low, too much heat loses to the environment, while too much electrical efficiency is sacrificed when PEG concentration is too high. When PEG concentration is 50%, even though some electrical efficiency is sacrificed, the thermal efficiency is enhanced significantly due to the absorption of the nanofluid filter. Besides, the highest total efficiency of 57.0% occurs at the PEG concentration of 75%. The values of MF with different heating times are illustrated in Fig. 7(b), which shows that increasing heating time does not always enhance MF, while the total efficiency shows a monotonically increasing function with the heating time, the highest MF value of 1.904 appears when the heating time is 20 min and the highest total efficiency of 58.8% occurs when the heating time is 40 min.

In the literature, several works have been done for nanofluid filters, the comparisons between the representative works from the literature and our results are carried out and shown in Fig. 7(c) and Table 4 . Huang et al. prepared Ag@SiO2 core-shell nanoparticles and dispersed them in propylene glycol-CoSO4 hybrid fluid, the MF value is as high as 1.69 [30]. Hjerrild et al. carried out the performance of aqueous nanofluids containing Ag@SiO2 core-shell nanoparticles and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [31], it is found that the addition of CNTs enhances the MF of nanofluid filters, while a relatively low MF value of 1.35 was found in this system compared with the work of Huang et al., which can be attributed to the better performance of propylene glycol-CoSO4 hybrid fluid than water as base fluid. An et al. dispersed Cu9S5 nanoparticles into oleylamine solution and obtained an MF value of 1.42 [26]. Cui et al. performed MgO aqueous nanofluids in a PV/T system, the MF was determined to be 1.55 [24]. Our results show that the CQD-nanofluid filters can achieve a high MF value of 1.904, which outperforms the reported nanofluid filters in the literature. From the analysis, we can conclude that CQD-nanofluid filters prepared in our work not only have the advantages of the simple preparation process and low cost but also can achieve high-quality PV/T conversion.

While Fig. 4 also indicates that the fabricated CQD-nanofluid filters in this work have relatively weak absorption in the infrared region, so searching for nanoparticles that show complementary with CQD nanofluids and can absorb the infrared light is a breach to improve the performance of CQD-nanofluid filters further in PV/T system.

In this paper, CQD nanofluids containing PEG and GL were synthesized through the microwave-heating method, which has ultra-stability over a long period. The prepared CQD nanofluids show excellent performance as SBSs in the PV/T system. CQD nanofluids absorb little light in the wavelengths between 650 and 1050 nm while having strong absorption in other wavelengths, which is exactly desired by silicon PV cells. The influence of the PEG concentration and heating time on the absorption of nanofluid filters and heat/electricity efficiencies of the PV/T system has been analyzed systematically. The absorption of CQD nanofluids is enhanced as the extension of heating time in the whole spectrum, which results in the decrease of the electrical output of PV cells and the increase of nanofluids’ stagnation temperature. Meanwhile, the increase in PEG concentration enhances the absorption of ultra-violet light while having little effect on the absorption in the infrared region. Finally, MF was introduced to assess the performance of nanofluid filters in PV/T systems, both PEG concentration and heating time have a significant effect on the value of MF. The results indicate that the nanofluid with a PEG concentration of 50% and heating time of 20 min has a peak MF value of 1.90. This work promotes the application of CQD nanofluids as SBS in PV/T systems.

Yang Xiao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing- Reviewing and Editing. Yanqiong Bao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing- Reviewing and Editing. Linfeng Yu: Data curation. Xiong Zheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing- Reviewing and Editing. Guangzhao Qin: Reviewing and Editing. Meijie Chen: Reviewing and Editing. Maogang He: Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52006059).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An evacuated receiver partially insulated by a solar transparent aerogel for parabolic trough collector
Energy Convers Manag
